title: SEGMENT 5 — Liver organoids: Takebe's organ buds and Clevers' hepatocytes

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — Liver organoids: Takebe's organ buds and Clevers' hepatocytes
The liver was an obvious next target after the intestine.

Liver disease is enormously common — hepatitis, fatty liver, cirrhosis, liver cancer all kill millions of people every year — and the liver, like the gut, is a relatively self-renewing organ with identifiable stem and progenitor cells.

If you could grow human liver tissue in a dish, you could study liver disease, screen drugs for liver toxicity (which is one of the leading causes of drug failure in development), and maybe even produce hepatocytes for cell-replacement therapy.
Two parallel approaches emerged in the early 2010s.
The first approach, from the Clevers group, was the direct extension of the intestinal protocol.

They isolated cells from adult human liver biopsies — specifically, ductal cells that express Lgr5 and similar markers — and showed that with an appropriate cocktail (Wnt, R-spondin, EGF, FGF, plus a couple of additional factors), these cells could form organoids that, depending on the conditions, could differentiate into hepatocyte-like cells, the major functional cell type of the liver.
These liver organoids could perform classical hepatocyte functions: they produced albumin, they metabolized drugs via cytochrome P450 enzymes, they stored glycogen.
They weren't quite as functional as primary human hepatocytes, but they were close, and crucially, they could be grown from a small biopsy and expanded over weeks to provide a lot of material.
The second approach, which made the bigger immediate splash, came from a young Japanese researcher named Takanori Takebe, working at Yokohama City University.
Takebe's 2013 paper in Nature — titled "Vascularized and functional human liver from an iPSC-derived organ bud transplant" — took a different approach.

Rather than starting from adult liver stem cells, Takebe used pluripotent stem cells differentiated into early hepatic endoderm — basically, the cell type from which liver buds first emerge during embryonic development.

Then he did something clever: he mixed those hepatic endoderm cells with two other cell types — endothelial cells (which form blood vessels) and mesenchymal stem cells (which provide supporting tissue).
And he didn't put them in Matrigel.
He just plated them on a soft surface and let them aggregate.
What happened was extraordinary.

The three cell types self-organized into a structure that looked, under the microscope, exactly like an embryonic liver bud — the structure that, in a normal embryo, forms when liver tissue first emerges from the foregut endoderm during the third week of development.
The hepatic endoderm formed the body of the bud, the endothelial cells started laying down vascular channels through it, and the mesenchymal cells provided the surrounding support.
It was a recapitulation, in a dish, of an early developmental event.
Then Takebe did something even better.
He transplanted these liver buds into mice — into the abdomen, where they had access to the host's blood supply.
The transplanted buds matured.
The endothelial cells in the buds connected to the host's blood vessels, forming functional perfusion.
And the hepatocyte-like cells started producing human albumin, detectable in the mouse's blood, indicating that human liver tissue was functioning inside a mouse.
Even better, when the mice were given a hepatotoxic injury, the transplanted human liver tissue protected them from dying.
The mini-livers were doing real work.
The Takebe paper was important for two reasons.
First, it showed that organoids could be transplanted and integrated into a host organ system, with proper vascularization.

The vascularization problem — which we'll come back to — is the biggest single limitation of organoids, and Takebe's approach of co-culturing with endothelial cells and then transplanting into a host was a way to sidestep it.
Second, it demonstrated that the organ bud stage of development was a powerful starting point.

Rather than trying to grow a fully mature organoid in a dish, you could grow the early developmental precursor and then let it mature in vivo, after transplant, using the host's circulatory system as the maturation engine.
The Takebe approach has since been extended to other organs — pancreas, kidney, intestine — using similar three-cell-type co-cultures.
The "organ bud" paradigm sits alongside the "epithelial organoid" paradigm as one of the two main approaches in the field today.
Liver organoids have been used for some genuinely important work.
They've been used to model alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, a genetic liver disease, by deriving organoids from patient iPSCs and showing the characteristic protein-trafficking defect.
They've been used to model fatty liver disease by exposing organoids to high concentrations of fatty acids and watching them develop lipid droplets and inflammation.

They've been used in drug screening — liver organoids are now part of many pharmaceutical companies' early hepatotoxicity panels, replacing some of the work that used to require primary human hepatocytes (which are scarce, expensive, and variable).
And there are early efforts to use liver organoids for cell replacement therapy in patients with end-stage liver disease, though those efforts are still in the preclinical phase.

The frontier here is to get organoids that combine hepatocytes with the full complement of supporting cell types — endothelial cells, Kupffer cells (the liver's resident macrophages), hepatic stellate cells (which mediate fibrosis), and bile duct epithelium.
Most current liver organoids are dominated by one or two cell types.
Getting the full menagerie of liver cells, arranged in proper architecture, with vasculature, is still a work in progress.
But it's progressing.
